model_narrative: A farmer allocates a fixed amount of land among several crops for the coming season. For each crop the farmer chooses how many acres to plant, and the planted acres turn into a harvest. Harvested crops can be sold, and for some crops the farmer may also buy additional quantities from outside to meet on-farm needs. Sugarbeets in particular can be sold through two channels paying different prices. The goal is to maximize total profit, which is the revenue from everything sold minus the cost of planting and the cost of buying crops in from outside.

sets.name: crop
sets.doc: the crops that can be grown on the farm; every crop in this set can be planted and produces a harvest

sets.name: cropr
sets.doc: the crops for which the farm must satisfy a minimum requirement for its own use and which may be bought in from outside; these are a subset of the grown crops, and sugarbeets are not among them

sets.name: cropx
sets.doc: the sales channels through which harvested crops can be sold; wheat and corn each have a single channel that shares the crop's name, while sugarbeets are sold through two separate price channels named beets1 and beets2 representing a favorable-price tier and a lower-price tier

params.name: yield_param
params.doc: the harvest yield of each crop, in tons per acre planted

params.name: plantcost
params.doc: the planting cost of each crop, in dollars per acre

params.name: sellprice
params.doc: the selling price obtained through each sales channel, in dollars per ton

params.name: purchprice
params.doc: the purchase price paid to buy in each requirement crop from outside, in dollars per ton

params.name: minreq
params.doc: the minimum quantity of each requirement crop the farm must have available for its own use, in tons

params.name: maxbuy
params.doc: the largest quantity of each requirement crop that may be purchased from outside, in tons

params.name: land
params.doc: the total acreage of land available on the farm, in acres

params.name: maxbeets1
params.doc: the largest quantity of sugarbeets that may be sold through the favorable-price channel beets1 before the lower-price channel must be used, in tons

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the acres of land devoted to each crop

vars.name: w
vars.doc: the tons sold through each sales channel

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the tons of each requirement crop purchased from outside

vars.name: yld
vars.doc: the tons harvested of each crop

vars.name: profit
vars.doc: the objective value variable for total profit

constraints.name: landuse
constraints.intent: The land the farmer plants is limited. Across all crops, the total acres devoted to planting cannot be more than the total acreage available on the farm.

constraints.name: yld[c]
constraints.intent: Harvest follows from how much is planted. For each crop, the tons harvested equal the crop's yield per acre multiplied by the acres planted of that crop.

constraints.name: yld[r]
constraints.intent: For each requirement crop the farm must end up with at least its minimum needed quantity available for its own use. The quantity available is what was harvested of that crop plus what was bought in from outside, less whatever amount of that crop was sold off through its sales channel. That available quantity must be at least the minimum required.

constraints.name: y[r]
constraints.intent: There is a ceiling on outside purchasing. For each requirement crop, the tons bought in from outside cannot exceed the purchase limit for that crop.

constraints.name: beets
constraints.intent: Sugarbeets are sold through two separate price channels, and together they cannot sell more than was grown. The combined tons sold through the favorable channel and the lower channel cannot exceed the tons of sugarbeets harvested.

constraints.name: beets1_limit
constraints.intent: Sales through the favorable sugarbeet price channel are capped. The tons sold through that favorable channel cannot exceed its allowed quantity.

query: The agricultural council is rolling out a crop-rotation mandate next season — corn and sugar beets together can't take up more than 70% of our total cultivable land, since they hit the soil hardest. We're evaluating the impact now. Plug that in and tell me where the planting mix and profit land.

query: There's a proposal on the table to sign a fixed corn-acreage commitment with the local co-op — at least 120 acres of corn each season for processing supply. If we lock in 120 acres of corn, what does the optimizer return for the plan and profit?

query: We're considering a supply-chain hedge where we buy in at least 50 tons of corn from the spot market rather than growing every ton ourselves. If we commit to purchasing 50 tons of corn externally, where does the plan and profit settle?

query: We're growing every kernel of wheat ourselves when the spot market is right there. Couldn't we require that purchased wheat cover at least 20% of the feed minimum? Wouldn't freeing those acres up for sugar beets pencil out better?

query: Beets are eating well over half the farm and that worries me for the soil long-term. Couldn't we hold sugar-beet acreage to at most 40% of our total land so the rotation isn't so beet-heavy? What's driving the model to pile so much ground into beets?
